(require "T2.rkt")

# CC4101 Tarea 2

## Parsing e Intérpretes

Para la resolución de la tarea recuerde que

                   Toda función debe estar acompañada de su firma, una breve descripción coloquial (en T2.rkt) y un conjunto significativo de tests (en test.rkt).
                   Todo datatype definido por el usuario (via deftype) debe estar acompañado de
                   una breve descripción coloquial y de la gramática BNF que lo genera.

              Si la función o el datatype no cumple con estas reglas, será ignorado.

         Ejercicio 1                                                                         22 Pt
            Se define un lenguaje aritmético cuyos programas denotan polinomios, en lugar de
            números. La sintaxis abstracta del lenguaje está dada por la siguiente gramática BNF:
               #|
               <poly> ::= (poly (Listof <number>))
                        | (id <id>)
                        | (add <poly> <poly>)
                        | (mul <poly> <poly>)
                        | (if0 <poly> <poly> <poly>)
                        | (with <id> <poly> <poly>)
               |#

               Un polinomio está representado por una lista de números, en donde cada posición
               contiene el coeficiente correspondiente a ese grado. Por ejemplo, (poly '(4 0 5)) representa al polinomio 4 + 5x2 .
               Dentro de las construcciones del lenguaje están los identificadores, la suma y el producto de polinomios, un condicional que compara la guarda con el polinomio nulo
               ((poly '(0))) y la definición local que asocia un identificador con un polinomio para
               que pueda ser referenciado en el cuerpo de la misma.
                (a) [6 Pt] Defina el tipo de datos recursivo Poly que genera los AST de los programas
                    del lenguaje.

                (b) [6 Pt] Defina la sintaxis concreta del lenguaje y escriba la función
                    ;; parse ::   <s-poly> -> Poly
                    que devuelve los programas escritos en sintaxis abstracta. Para ello, considere
                    los siguientes ejemplos:

        >>> (parse 0)
        (poly '(0))
        >>> (parse '(3 1 0 0))
        (poly '(3 1))
        >>> (parse 'x)
        (id 'x)
        >>> (parse '(+ (3 5 7) (1 2)))
        (add (poly '(3 5 7)) (poly '(1 2)))
        >>> (parse '(* (2 1) (1 2)))
        (mul (poly '(2 1)) (poly '(1 2)))
        >>> (parse '(if0 (+ (3 5) (-3 2)) 1 0))
        (if0 (add (poly '(3 5)) (poly '(-3 2))) (poly '(1)) (poly '(0)))
        >>> (parse '(with x (1 2) (* 2 x)))
        (with 'x (poly '(1 2)) (mul (poly '(2)) (id 'x)))

    (c) [10 Pt] Sea la estructura Env que modela un ambiente en donde se tienen las
        relaciones entre los identificadores y sus valores en forma de polinomios puros,
        es decir, sin operaciones y únicamente formado por el constructor poly. Defina
        la función
        ;; reduce ::   Poly Env -> Poly
        que devuelve un polinomio puro.
        >>> (reduce (poly '(3 1 0)) empty-env)
        (poly '(3 1))
        >>> (reduce (id 'x) empty-env)
        reduce: variable x is not defined
        >>> (reduce (id 'x) (extend-env x (poly '(1)) empty-env))
        (poly '(1))
        >>> (reduce (add (poly '(3 5 7)) (poly '(1 2))) empty-env)
        (poly '(4 7 7)

Ejercicio 2                                                                          38 Pt
   El archivo T2.rkt contiene una definición inicial del tipo de datos recursivo Expr, basado en el lenguaje visto en clases.
    (a) [6 Pt] Extienda Expr (y su gramática BNF) con operadores números reales e
        imaginarios. Además, agregue una forma de introducir varias definiciones locales
        en una sola expresión, es decir, una forma de with con varias definiciones. Para
        ello, utilice los siguientes ejemplos como guía (no es necesario implementar la
        función parser aún):
             Números reales e imaginarios:
        >>> (parser '1)
        ( real 1)
        >>> (parser '(1 i ))
        (imaginary 1)
        >>> (parser '(+ 2 (1 i )))
        (addc (real 2) (imaginary 1))
            Identificadores y definiciones locales:

    >>> (parser '(with [ (x 1) (y 1)] (+ x y)))
    (withc (list (cons 'x ( real 1)) (cons 'y ( real 1))) (addc (idc 'x) (idc 'y)))

(b) [6 Pt] Implemente la función parser siguiendo los ejemplos anteriores. Además,
    considere que la expresión with siempre debe recibir al menos una definición y
    en caso contrario se debe lanzar una excepción:
    >>> (parser '(with [ ] 1))
    parser: *with* expects at least one definition

(c) [6 Pt] En el archivo base, se encuentra una definición de valores CValue para
    el lenguaje. Dado que este trabaja con números reales e imaginarios, naturalmente los valores son los números complejos. Implemente la función from-CValue
    que convierte un CValue a un elemento Expr. Además, implemente las funciones
    cmplx+, cmplx- y cmplx0. Las primeras dos funciones suman y restan, respectivamente, dos valores del lenguaje. La última función retorna #t si el número
    complejo es 0.
    >>> (cmplx+ (compV 1 2) (compV 3 4))
    (compV 4 6)
    >>> (cmplx- (compV 1 2) (compV 3 4))
    (compV -2 -2)
    >>> (cmplx0? (compV 0 1))
    #f

(d) [10 Pt] Implemente la función subst que realiza la substitución de una expresión
    por un identificador. Note que una expresión with introduce identificadores que
    pueden ser utilizados en próximas definiciones, permitiendo que expresiones como
    la siguiente sean válidas:
    (with [ (x 2) (y (+ x 1))] (+ x y))
    La definición de y ocupa x.
    Su función de substitución debe tomar esto en cuenta y evitar substituir variables
    que han sido "oscurecidas" por otra definición local. Por ejemplo:
    ;; Este ejemplo no tiene "shadowing"
    >>> (subst (parser '(with [ (x 2) (y z) ] (+ x z))) 'z ( real 1))
    (withc (list (cons 'x ( real 2)) (cons 'y ( real 1))) (addc (idc 'x) ( real 1)))
    ;; En este ejemplo si ocurre "shadowing" -> no se realiza la substitucion
    >>> (subst (parser '(with [ (x 2) (y x)] (+ x x))) 'x ( real 1))
    (withc (list (cons 'x ( real 2)) (cons 'y (idc 'x))) (addc (idc 'x) (idc 'x)))
    Hint: Le puede ser útil definir una función auxiliar que realice una substitución
    sobre una lista de definiciones.

(e) [10 Pt] Implemente la función interp que reduce una expresión (Expr) en un
    valor del lenguaje (CValue). Si se encuentra una variable libre, arroje un error
    con el mensaje: "Free occurrence of a variable".

**Considering AST constructors**

I need to determine the exact names of AST constructors for the `Poly` type in P1. I think using `deftype`, like `(poly l) (id s) ...`, with pattern matching might be the way to go, but I wonder if `deftype` creates predicates or accessors automatically — probably not necessary. I need to implement parse and reduce while looking at existing type definitions too. I'm considering the concrete syntax for P1 parse and how to handle trimming zeros in lists.

**Handling numbers and symbols**

I need to manage plain numbers and symbols for polynomial operations. It seems that lists starting with symbols like `+` or `*` should be parsed in function. For example, a parse might look like this: `(parse '(+ (3 5 7) (1 2)))`, which involves recursive parsing. I also need to consider the `with` form, like `(with x (1 2) (* 2 x))`, ensuring the correct handling of symbols that come after the `with`.

**Considering polynomial representation**

I'm thinking I need to represent a polynomial list as a list of numbers. It seems there's no syntax to represent an empty list, so I’ll need a helper function to trim zeros. For multiplication, standard convolution should work, but when the input lists have mismatched lengths, I have to extend with zeros and then trim the result. Oh, this should also work for constants. Additionally, I might implement `reduce` for `add` and `mul` using recursive lookups. I need to modify the Expr type and support real and imaginary numbers in the parser, perhaps using specific syntax for numbers and lists.

**Parsing new expressions**

I'm working through how to handle cases where the first element is negative, likely indicating a number. I need to allow the 'i' symbol only as the second element, like in a quoted list example `'(1 i)`. If the list has two elements and the second is 'i, then it should be parsed as imaginary. I also have to think about handling empty lists. 

I need to create a type `Expr`, possibly with a new constructor for definitions. For `defs`, I might use a list of pairs. I should define a proper grammar in BNF for the new expression type while also updating the comment for `Expr`.

**Error handling and conversions**

I need the parser to throw an error when there are no definitions in the `with` statement, using the exact message: "parser: *with* expects at least one definition." Now, I'm considering how to convert `CValue` to `Expr`, which could return a `real`, `imaginary`, or possibly an `addc`.

Since `CValue` represents complex numbers, I should aim for an expression that reflects its value. If the imaginary part is zero, I can represent it as `(real r)`, and if the real part is zero, then `(imaginary i)`. 

But what about the case when both parts are non-zero? The expression language might not have a direct way to represent general complex numbers. It seems I could represent this using `addc` with the real and imaginary components combined. It's crucial to ensure that `from-CValue` correctly maps these representations to `Expr`.

**Translating complex values**

I could translate `compV r i` to `addc (real r) (imaginary i)`, but if one component is zero, I might simplify that representation. I’m considering that in this context, the original language likely deals with complex values and expressions that are `real` and `imaginary` numeric literals.

It seems there might be an evaluator where values can convert to expressions, allowing for substitution. I should search the documentation for more insight, and inspecting `T2.rkt` could also reveal helpful information, although I'm unsure if `from-CValue` interacts with `subst`.

**Clarifying interpreter semantics**

I need to understand the entire interpreter's semantics better. It seems like the real and imaginary constructors function more like value expressions than arithmetic operations. For example, when parsing `(parser '(1 i))`, it indicates that the expression `(1 i)` represents the imaginary number 1i. So, the `imaginary` constructor probably holds the coefficient of i. I think `from-CValue` converts values into equivalent expressions, representing complex numbers as sums of their components.

**Considering substitutions and definitions**

I think substitution is key when using value expressions, and it makes sense to call `from-CValue` after evaluating definitions. The syntax for subst takes three arguments: `Expr`, `Symbol`, and another `Expr`, as shown in examples like `(subst ... 'z (real 1))`. It seems `interp` may return `CValue` directly, while `from-CValue` could represent computed constants. I need to implement arithmetic operations and manage shadowing for sequential semantics in definitions. It's crucial that substitutions correctly reflect earlier definitions in sequences, like in the `(with [...])` example.

**Handling substitution with shadowing**

When substituting a variable like x with some expression, I need to ensure that I don't substitute under shadowed variables. In the example `(with [(x 2) (y x)] (+ x x))`, substituting x with 1 shouldn't affect occurrences in the body or in y. Since x is shadowed by the first definition, the local x should take precedence, meaning the substitution will only refer to the local variable and won't alter later occurrences after the shadowing.

**Evaluating variable occurrence issue**

I’m considering an error I encountered regarding the "Free occurrence of a variable." I think I might need to evaluate definitions sequentially and explore substitution. I could interpret this sequentially with environments, but there isn't an environment datatype defined. A simpler approach might be to evaluate definitions and substitute into the body. I'll need to ensure that I'm mapping symbols appropriately since there's no environment type. Using an association list could be a valid way to implement this inside the interpreter helper.

**Examining expression constructors**

It seems like the user expects new expression constructors like `real`, `imaginary`, `idc`, `addc`, `subc`, `if0c`, and `withc` due to the expression language now accommodating complex numbers. I wonder if the original constructors were different, like `idc` and `num`. I need to check out the entire skeleton to clarify this. So, I’ll inspect the context around the `Expr` comments in T2.rkt, which indicate new constructors are expected to extend the existing type.

**Considering Racket patterns**

I’m trying to figure out whether to use `(list '+ l r)` or just `(list '+ l r)` in Racket. Symbols are treated as literals when written literally, but in patterns, do I need to quote them? I think `'( + )` isn't necessary. However, when I use the pattern `[(list '+ l r) ...]`, it might interpret `+` as a variable. I need to clarify how these symbols behave in different contexts.

**Parsing real numbers**

I'm thinking through how to parse the number '1. It seems like it's a real number, but if it appears as a list, like '(1), should it still be interpreted as real? The examples show complex numbers with lists, but I haven't seen '(1) treated the same way. The parser uses '1 to translate to (real 1), while '(1 i) goes to (imaginary 1). I'm wondering if lists of numbers are only for complex forms. This needs more thought!

**Interpreting numeric lists**

I'm analyzing how to parse numeric values. It looks like a single number atom is interpreted as real, while a list of two numbers with 'i' is seen as imaginary. But what about a singleton list like '(1)? Should that parse to real too? It's a bit ambiguous since it could look like a parenthesized expression. I might treat singleton lists as real numbers for convenience, but examples for P1 use lists as polynomials. I need to be careful with broader interpretations!

**Considering robust parsing**

I’m thinking that to be more robust, I might accept '(1) as real 1. But then again, that could confuse users since it looks like a parenthesis around a number in Scheme. Yet if they typed '(1), it’s just a list of one number. I don’t have an example for this either. In standard Racket, '(1) is recognized as a singleton list. If the task only expects '(1 i), then maybe '(1) shouldn’t even be considered.

**Considering implementation details**

I’m thinking that by asking to implement from-CValue separately, it might be intended for use when turning the evaluated result back to syntax, like in tests. I’d lean toward having conditions such as: if `i=0`, then output (real `r`); if `r=0`, then output (imaginary `i`); or else, add (real `r`) and (imaginary `i`). If both are zero, then output (real 0). This approach feels generic yet reasonable!

**Evaluating substitution implementation**

I see that using recursive traversal for `subst` might simplify the process. I could implement a helper function, `subst-defs`, to manage the list of definitions sequentially, accounting for shadowing. Alternatively, a recursive function could check expressions and a flag indicating whether a target has been shadowed. This flag, `blocked?`, would help determine if prior bindings of the same name interfere. It's all about making things clearer and more efficient!

**Investigating testing options**

I need to determine if T2_tests.rkt only requires a file. It looks like I need to implement some significant tests. Should I be using check-expect? I have to figure out the right testing framework, maybe `test`? It would help to inspect the existing language. Since T2_tests.rkt is empty aside from the require statement, I think I can create tests using either `test` or `check-expect`. I might want to read the package documentation to clarify the options available.

#lang play


#|
Nombre: 
¿Utilizó Whiteboard Policy? (SI o NO):
En caso afirmativo, ¿con quién?:
¿en qué ejercicio(s)?:
|#


;;------------ ;;
;;==== P1 ==== ;;
;;------------ ;;

#| Parte A |#

#|
<poly> ::= (poly (Listof <number>))
         | (id <id>)
         | (add <poly> <poly>)
         | (mul <poly> <poly>)
         | (if0 <poly> <poly> <poly>)
         | (with <id> <poly> <poly>)
|#

;; Poly :: AST de programas polinomiales.
;; <poly> ::= (poly (Listof <number>)) | (id <id>) | (add <poly> <poly>) | (mul <poly> <poly>) | (if0 <poly> <poly> <poly>) | (with <id> <poly> <poly>)
(deftype Poly
  (poly coeffs)
  (id x)
  (add l r)
  (mul l r)
  (if0 c t f)
  (with x rhs body))

#| Parte B |#

#|
<s-poly> ::= <number>
           | <symbol>
           | (list <number> ...)
           | (list + <s-poly> <s-poly> ...)
           | (list * <s-poly> <s-poly> ...)
           | (list if0 <s-poly> <s-poly> <s-poly>)
           | (list with <symbol> <s-poly> <s-poly>)
|#

;; parse :: <s-poly> -> Poly
;; Convierte la sintaxis concreta del lenguaje de polinomios a su AST.
(define (parse s-poly)
  (cond
    [(number? s-poly) (poly (trim-trailing-zeros (list s-poly)))]
    [(symbol? s-poly) (id s-poly)]
    [(and (list? s-poly) (andmap number? s-poly))
     (poly (trim-trailing-zeros s-poly))]
    [else
     (match s-poly
       [(list '+ l r) (add (parse l) (parse r))]
       [(list '* l r) (mul (parse l) (parse r))]
       [(list 'if0 c t f) (if0 (parse c) (parse t) (parse f))]
       [(list 'with x rhs body) (with x (parse rhs) (parse body))]
       [else (error 'parse "invalid s-poly syntax")])]))

#| Parte C |#

#|
<env> ::= (tyEnv)
        | (xtEnv <symbol> <poly> <env>)
|#
(deftype Env
  (tyEnv)
  (xtEnv symbol value env))

(define empty-env (tyEnv))

;; extend-env :: symbol Poly Env -> Env
;; Agrega una nueva asociación al ambiente.
(define (extend-env x v env)
  (xtEnv x v env))

;; env-lookup :: symbol Env -> (or/c #f Poly)
;; Busca una variable en un ambiente; retorna #f si no está.
(define (env-lookup x env)
  (match env
    [(tyEnv) #f]
    [(xtEnv s v tail) (if (symbol=? x s)
                          v
                          (env-lookup x tail))]))

;; trim-trailing-zeros :: (Listof Number) -> (Listof Number)
;; Elimina ceros al final de la lista de coeficientes.
(define (trim-trailing-zeros coeffs)
  (let loop ([rev (reverse coeffs)])
    (cond
      [(empty? rev) (list 0)]
      [(and (zero? (first rev)) (empty? (rest rev))) (list 0)]
      [(zero? (first rev)) (loop (rest rev))]
      [else (reverse rev)])))

;; zeros :: Number -> (Listof Number)
;; Construye una lista de ceros de largo n.
(define (zeros n)
  (if (zero? n)
      '()
      (cons 0 (zeros (sub1 n)))))

;; pad-right :: (Listof Number) Number -> (Listof Number)
;; Completa con ceros a la derecha hasta alcanzar el largo pedido.
(define (pad-right xs extra)
  (append xs (zeros extra)))

;; update-at :: (Listof Number) Number Number -> (Listof Number)
;; Suma un incremento en la posición dada de una lista.
(define (update-at xs idx delta)
  (cond
    [(empty? xs) '()]
    [(zero? idx) (cons (+ (first xs) delta) (rest xs))]
    [else (cons (first xs) (update-at (rest xs) (sub1 idx) delta))]))

;; poly-add :: Poly Poly -> Poly
;; Suma dos polinomios ya reducidos.
(define (poly-add p1 p2)
  (match* (p1 p2)
    [((poly c1) (poly c2))
     (define len1 (length c1))
     (define len2 (length c2))
     (define maxlen (max len1 len2))
     (define d1 (pad-right c1 (- maxlen len1)))
     (define d2 (pad-right c2 (- maxlen len2)))
     (poly (trim-trailing-zeros (map + d1 d2)))]))

;; poly-mul :: Poly Poly -> Poly
;; Multiplica dos polinomios ya reducidos.
(define (poly-mul p1 p2)
  (match* (p1 p2)
    [((poly c1) (poly c2))
     (define len1 (length c1))
     (define len2 (length c2))
     (define result-len (- (+ len1 len2) 1))
     (define (inner a ys j acc)
       (cond
         [(empty? ys) acc]
         [else (inner a (rest ys) (add1 j)
                      (update-at acc j (* a (first ys))))]))
     (define (outer xs i acc)
       (cond
         [(empty? xs) acc]
         [else (outer (rest xs) (add1 i) (inner (first xs) c2 0 acc))]))
     (poly (trim-trailing-zeros (outer c1 0 (zeros result-len))))]))

;; poly-zero? :: Poly -> Boolean
;; Reconoce el polinomio nulo.
(define (poly-zero? p)
  (match p
    [(poly coeffs) (andmap zero? coeffs)]))

;; reduce :: Poly Env -> Poly
;; Reduce una expresión polinomial a un polinomio puro.
(define (reduce p env)
  (match p
    [(poly coeffs) (poly (trim-trailing-zeros coeffs))]
    [(id x)
     (let ([ans (env-lookup x env)])
       (if ans
           ans
           (error 'reduce (string-append "variable " (symbol->string x) " is not defined"))))]
    [(add l r) (poly-add (reduce l env) (reduce r env))]
    [(mul l r) (poly-mul (reduce l env) (reduce r env))]
    [(if0 c t f)
     (if (poly-zero? (reduce c env))
         (reduce t env)
         (reduce f env))]
    [(with x rhs body)
     (reduce body (extend-env x (reduce rhs env) env))]))


;;------------ ;;
;;==== P2 ==== ;;
;;------------ ;;

#| Parte A |#

#|
<expr> ::= (real <number>)
        | (imaginary <number>)
        | (idc <symbol>)
        | (addc <expr> <expr>)
        | (subc <expr> <expr>)
        | (if0c <expr> <expr> <expr>)
        | (withc (Listof (cons <symbol> <expr>)) <expr>)
|#
(deftype Expr
  (real n)
  (imaginary i)
  (idc x)
  (addc l r)
  (subc l r)
  (if0c c t f)
  (withc defs body))

#| Parte B |#

#|
Concrete syntax of expressions:

<s-expr> ::= <number>
           | <symbol>
           | (+ <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (- <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (if0 <s-expr> <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (with [<def> ...] <s-expr>)
<def>    ::= (<symbol> <s-expr>)
|#

;; parser :: <s-expr> -> Expr
;; Traduce la sintaxis concreta del lenguaje complejo a AST.
(define (parser s-expr)
  (cond
    [(number? s-expr) (real s-expr)]
    [(symbol? s-expr) (idc s-expr)]
    [(and (list? s-expr)
          (= (length s-expr) 2)
          (number? (first s-expr))
          (symbol? (second s-expr))
          (symbol=? (second s-expr) 'i))
     (imaginary (first s-expr))]
    [else
     (match s-expr
       [(list '+ l r) (addc (parser l) (parser r))]
       [(list '- l r) (subc (parser l) (parser r))]
       [(list 'if0 c t f) (if0c (parser c) (parser t) (parser f))]
       [(list 'with defs body)
        (cond
          [(empty? defs) (error 'parser "*with* expects at least one definition")]
          [else (withc (parse-defs defs) (parser body))])]
       [else (error 'parser "invalid expression")])]))

;; parse-defs :: (Listof <def>) -> (Listof (cons Symbol Expr))
;; Convierte la lista de definiciones locales a la representación interna.
(define (parse-defs defs)
  (match defs
    ['() '()]
    [(cons def rest)
     (match def
       [(list name rhs)
        (cons (cons name (parser rhs)) (parse-defs rest))]
       [else (error 'parser "invalid with definition")])]))

#| Parte C |#

#|
<cvalue> ::= (compV <num> <num>)
|#
(deftype CValue (compV r i))

;; from-CValue :: CValue -> Expr
;; Convierte un valor complejo en una expresión equivalente.
(define (from-CValue v)
  (match v
    [(compV r i)
     (cond
       [(and (zero? r) (zero? i)) (real 0)]
       [(zero? i) (real r)]
       [(zero? r) (imaginary i)]
       [else (addc (real r) (imaginary i))])]))

;; cmplx+ :: CValue CValue -> CValue
;; Suma dos valores complejos.
(define (cmplx+ v1 v2)
  (match* (v1 v2)
    [((compV r1 i1) (compV r2 i2)) (compV (+ r1 r2) (+ i1 i2))]))

;; cmplx- :: CValue CValue -> CValue
;; Resta dos valores complejos.
(define (cmplx- v1 v2)
  (match* (v1 v2)
    [((compV r1 i1) (compV r2 i2)) (compV (- r1 r2) (- i1 i2))]))

;; cmplx0? :: CValue -> Boolean
;; Indica si el complejo es exactamente cero.
(define (cmplx0? v)
  (match v
    [(compV r i) (and (zero? r) (zero? i))]))

#| Parte D |#

;; subst :: Expr Symbol Expr -> Expr
;; Sustituye apariciones libres de un identificador por otra expresión.
(define (subst in what for)
  (define (subst* e blocked?)
    (match e
      [(real n) e]
      [(imaginary n) e]
      [(idc x) (if (and (not blocked?) (symbol=? x what)) for e)]
      [(addc l r) (addc (subst* l blocked?) (subst* r blocked?))]
      [(subc l r) (subc (subst* l blocked?) (subst* r blocked?))]
      [(if0c c t f) (if0c (subst* c blocked?) (subst* t blocked?) (subst* f blocked?))]
      [(withc defs body)
       (let-values ([(defs* blocked2) (subst-defs defs blocked?)])
         (withc defs* (subst* body blocked2)))]))
  (define (subst-defs defs blocked?)
    (match defs
      ['() (values '() blocked?)]
      [(cons def rest)
       (match def
         [(cons name rhs)
          (define rhs* (subst* rhs blocked?))
          (define blocked2 (or blocked? (symbol=? name what)))
          (let-values ([(rest* blocked3) (subst-defs rest blocked2)])
            (values (cons (cons name rhs*) rest*) blocked3))]
         [else (error 'subst "invalid with definition")])]))
  (subst* in #f))

#| Parte E |#

;; interp :: Expr -> CValue
;; Evalúa una expresión compleja y retorna su valor.
(define (interp expr)
  (define (lookup x env)
    (match env
      ['() (error 'interp "Free occurrence of a variable")]
      [(cons (cons y v) rest) (if (symbol=? x y) v (lookup x rest))]))
  (define (extend-env x v env)
    (cons (cons x v) env))
  (define (interp/env e env)
    (match e
      [(real n) (compV n 0)]
      [(imaginary i) (compV 0 i)]
      [(idc x) (lookup x env)]
      [(addc l r) (cmplx+ (interp/env l env) (interp/env r env))]
      [(subc l r) (cmplx- (interp/env l env) (interp/env r env))]
      [(if0c c t f)
       (if (cmplx0? (interp/env c env))
           (interp/env t env)
           (interp/env f env))]
      [(withc defs body)
       (interp/env body (interp-defs defs env))]))
  (define (interp-defs defs env)
    (match defs
      ['() env]
      [(cons def rest)
       (match def
         [(cons name rhs)
          (define v (interp/env rhs env))
          (interp-defs rest (extend-env name v env))]
         [else (error 'interp "invalid with definition")])]))
  (interp/env expr '()))

;; trim-trailing-zeros :: (Listof Number) -> (Listof Number)
;; Elimina ceros al final de la lista de coeficientes.
(define (trim-trailing-zeros coeffs)
  (let loop ([rev (reverse coeffs)])
    (cond
      [(empty? rev) (list 0)]
      [(and (zero? (first rev)) (empty? (rest rev))) (list 0)]
      [(zero? (first rev)) (loop (rest rev))]
      [else (reverse rev)])))

;; zeros :: Number -> (Listof Number)
;; Construye una lista de ceros de largo n.
(define (zeros n)
  (if (zero? n)
      '()
      (cons 0 (zeros (sub1 n)))))

;; pad-right :: (Listof Number) Number -> (Listof Number)
;; Completa con ceros a la derecha hasta alcanzar el largo pedido.
(define (pad-right xs extra)
  (append xs (zeros extra)))

;; update-at :: (Listof Number) Number Number -> (Listof Number)
;; Suma un incremento en la posición dada de una lista.
(define (update-at xs idx delta)
  (cond
    [(empty? xs) '()]
    [(zero? idx) (cons (+ (first xs) delta) (rest xs))]
    [else (cons (first xs) (update-at (rest xs) (sub1 idx) delta))]))
